title: The bus and the funeral
summary: A tourist in Mexico City is annoyed that his bus is rerouted. The driver explains the city he is annoyed at.

body.speaker: Narrator
body.text: Daniel, a tourist from Canada, has been in Mexico City for three days. His bus, on which he has been riding for forty minutes towards Coyoacán, has just been stopped by a traffic officer and redirected. The driver, Don Beto, has been doing this route for nineteen years. Daniel approaches the front of the bus.

body.speaker: Daniel
body.text: Excuse me. Why are we going this way? This is not the route on the map. I am already late. I have a museum reservation at four.

body.speaker: Don Beto
body.text: Yes, sir. There is a funeral procession on Avenida Universidad. The family is walking the coffin from the church to the cemetery. The whole street is closed for about twenty minutes.

body.speaker: Daniel
body.text: A funeral closes a public bus route?

body.speaker: Don Beto
body.text: Yes. It is normal here. The route is reopened as soon as the procession has passed. I will get you to Coyoacán about fifteen minutes late.

body.speaker: Daniel
body.text: I understand families want to mourn, but it is a working day. I have appointments. Is there really no way to make the family walk on a smaller street?

body.speaker: Don Beto
body.text: Sir, I will say this gently because you do not know our city yet. The family is not walking on Avenida Universidad to inconvenience you. They are walking on Avenida Universidad because the man who died lived on it for sixty years. He sold tortas at the corner I just turned away from. Many of the people walking behind the coffin ate his tortas every day of their working lives. The street is theirs, in a sense your map does not show.

body.speaker: Daniel
body.text: But I am also using the street. I paid for the bus.

body.speaker: Don Beto
body.text: Yes. You paid forty pesos for a bus ride, and you have one. The detour is part of the bus ride today. It is not extra. The fare includes weather, traffic, and, sometimes, a funeral. None of us know in the morning which it will be.

body.speaker: Daniel
body.text: You sound as if you are defending it.

body.speaker: Don Beto
body.text: I am explaining it. I have driven this bus for nineteen years. In nineteen years I have been stopped about thirty times for funerals. I have been stopped about four thousand times for road works, two thousand times for political demonstrations, six hundred times for parties — quinceañeras, weddings — and twice for the President. The funerals are not the largest interruption. They are only the one that surprises tourists.

body.speaker: Daniel
body.text: I have not been rude. I just want to know why I am late.

body.speaker: Don Beto
body.text: Of course. You are late because, in this city, the dead are still allowed to use the road. Most cities have decided that the dead must use a different road — a side street, a hearse, a quiet hour. We have not decided that yet. Some people think we should. Others think we have not decided that because we do not want to. I am one of the others.

body.speaker: Daniel
body.text: And when you yourself die, will your family walk you down Avenida Universidad?

body.speaker: Don Beto
body.text: If they want to, and if the route is closed for them, yes. I expect a passenger that day will be annoyed because he is late to a museum. I hope my granddaughter answers him the way I have answered you. Patiently, but without apologising.

body.speaker: Daniel
body.text: I am sorry I was sharp.

body.speaker: Don Beto
body.text: You were not sharp. You were on time. Time is what your country gave you. The funeral is what mine has given me. Both are gifts, in a way. We are both lucky.

body.speaker: Narrator
body.text: Daniel arrived at the museum twelve minutes late. He told the story that evening, at a small bar, to a Mexican friend. The friend asked him what he had said back to the driver. Daniel said: 'I thanked him.' The friend nodded. 'Then you got off the bus,' she said, 'in the city you came to see.'

annotations.meaningVi: lễ mừng sinh nhật 15 tuổi (truyền thống Mỹ Latinh)

grammarNotes.title: Present perfect for the running balance of experience
grammarNotes.bodyVi: B1 dùng present perfect để cộng dồn kinh nghiệm tới hiện tại. Trong bài: 'I have driven this bus for nineteen years.' 'In nineteen years I have been stopped about thirty times for funerals.' 'I have been stopped about four thousand times for road works...' (so sánh nhiều mốc tích luỹ). Đối lập với past simple chỉ một lần: 'Daniel arrived at the museum twelve minutes late.' (sự việc đơn lẻ trong quá khứ). Present perfect giúp diễn tả 'tới giờ tôi đã ... bao nhiêu lần'.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: B1 uses the present perfect for a running total of experience. In the text: 'I have driven this bus for nineteen years.' 'In nineteen years I have been stopped about thirty times for funerals.' 'I have been stopped about four thousand times for road works...' (multiple accumulated counts). Contrast with past simple for a single event: 'Daniel arrived at the museum twelve minutes late.' The present perfect lets you say 'how many times so far I have ...'.

translationVi:
Daniel, du khách Canada, đã ở Mexico City ba ngày. Xe buýt anh đang đi bốn mươi phút tới Coyoacán vừa bị một cảnh sát giao thông chặn và đổi hướng. Tài xế, Don Beto, lái tuyến này mười chín năm. Daniel đi lên đầu xe.

Daniel: Xin lỗi. Sao mình đi đường này? Đây không phải tuyến trên bản đồ. Tôi đã muộn. Tôi đặt bảo tàng lúc bốn giờ.

Don Beto: Vâng. Có đoàn đưa tang trên Avenida Universidad. Gia đình đang đưa quan tài từ nhà thờ tới nghĩa trang. Cả phố đóng khoảng hai mươi phút.

Daniel: Một đám tang đóng cả tuyến buýt công cộng?

Don Beto: Vâng. Ở đây bình thường. Sẽ mở lại ngay khi đoàn qua. Tôi sẽ đưa anh tới Coyoacán muộn khoảng mười lăm phút.

Daniel: Tôi hiểu gia đình muốn thương tiếc, nhưng đây là ngày làm việc. Tôi có lịch hẹn. Thật không có cách nào bắt gia đình đi phố nhỏ hơn?

Don Beto: Anh, tôi sẽ nói nhẹ vì anh chưa biết thành phố này. Gia đình không đi Avenida Universidad để gây phiền cho anh. Họ đi vì người mất đã sống trên phố đó sáu mươi năm. Ông bán tortas ở góc tôi vừa rẽ. Nhiều người đi sau quan tài đã ăn tortas của ông mỗi ngày trong đời lao động của mình. Phố là của họ, theo một nghĩa mà bản đồ của anh không cho thấy.

Daniel: Nhưng tôi cũng đang dùng phố. Tôi đã trả tiền vé.

Don Beto: Vâng. Anh trả bốn mươi peso cho một chuyến buýt, và anh có. Đường vòng là một phần của chuyến hôm nay. Không phải phụ thêm. Vé bao gồm thời tiết, giao thông, và đôi khi, đám tang. Không ai biết buổi sáng nay là thứ nào.

Daniel: Anh nói nghe như đang bênh nó.

Don Beto: Tôi đang giải thích. Tôi lái xe này mười chín năm. Trong mười chín năm tôi bị dừng khoảng ba mươi lần vì đám tang. Khoảng bốn nghìn lần vì sửa đường, hai nghìn lần vì biểu tình chính trị, sáu trăm lần vì tiệc — quinceañera, đám cưới — và hai lần vì Tổng thống. Đám tang không phải sự gián đoạn lớn nhất. Chỉ là cái làm khách du lịch ngạc nhiên.

Daniel: Tôi không thô lỗ. Tôi chỉ muốn biết vì sao muộn.

Don Beto: Tất nhiên. Anh muộn vì ở thành phố này, người chết vẫn được dùng đường. Hầu hết thành phố đã quyết định người chết phải dùng đường khác — phố phụ, xe tang, giờ vắng. Chúng tôi chưa quyết. Có người nghĩ nên. Người khác nghĩ chúng tôi chưa quyết vì không muốn. Tôi thuộc nhóm sau.

Daniel: Và khi chính anh mất, gia đình anh có đưa anh dọc Avenida Universidad không?

Don Beto: Nếu họ muốn, và nếu phố được đóng cho họ, có. Tôi đoán ngày đó sẽ có hành khách bực vì muộn bảo tàng. Tôi hy vọng cháu gái tôi đáp anh ta như tôi đáp anh. Kiên nhẫn, nhưng không xin lỗi.

Daniel: Xin lỗi tôi cộc.

Don Beto: Anh không cộc. Anh đúng giờ. Thời gian là điều đất nước anh đã cho anh. Đám tang là điều đất nước tôi đã cho tôi. Cả hai theo cách nào đó là món quà. Cả hai chúng ta đều may.

Daniel tới bảo tàng muộn mười hai phút. Tối đó anh kể chuyện ở một quán bar nhỏ với một người bạn Mexico. Bạn anh hỏi anh đã đáp tài xế thế nào. Daniel nói: 'Tôi đã cảm ơn ông ấy.' Người bạn gật đầu. 'Vậy là anh đã xuống xe,' cô nói, 'ở chính thành phố anh tới để xem.'

options: Daniel's bus to Coyoacán is rerouted by traffic police; he is annoyed about being late for a 4 pm museum reservation
A funeral procession from the church to the cemetery, ~20 minutes

questions.prompt: Who is the man who died?
options: A torta-seller who lived on Avenida Universidad for 60 years; many mourners ate his food daily for years

questions.prompt: How does Don Beto frame the detour?
options: The fare includes weather, traffic, and sometimes a funeral — none known in the morning

questions.prompt: What statistics does Don Beto give for 19 years?
options: ~30 funerals; ~4,000 road works; ~2,000 demonstrations; ~600 parties; 2 presidents

questions.prompt: Why does Don Beto think tourists notice funerals?
options: Funerals are not the largest interruption — only the one that surprises tourists

questions.prompt: What philosophical claim does Don Beto make about the dead and roads?
options: Most cities have decided the dead must use a different road; Mexico City has not — some think we should, some think we don't want to
questions.explanation: Don Beto's 'the dead are still allowed' speech.

questions.prompt: What does Don Beto imagine for his own funeral?
options: If family wants and the route is closed, he is walked down the avenue; expects a future passenger to be annoyed; hopes his granddaughter answers patiently without apologising

questions.prompt: How does Don Beto reframe Daniel's emotion?
options: 'You were not sharp. You were on time. Time is what your country gave you. The funeral is what mine has given me.'
questions.hint: Don Beto's closing line to Daniel.

questions.prompt: How late was Daniel in the end?
What did Daniel's Mexican friend say?
options: 'Then you got off the bus in the city you came to see.'
A short delay can be a window into a city's whole philosophy of time and the dead — and a foreigner who lets it teach him meets the place he came to see.
Drivers should not talk to passengers.
questions.hint: Don Beto's reframe and the final friend.

cloze.template:
Narrator: Daniel, a tourist from Canada, has been in Mexico City for three days. His bus, on which he has been riding for forty minutes towards Coyoacán, has just been stopped by a traffic officer and redirected. The driver, Don Beto, has been doing this route for {{b1}} years. Daniel approaches the front of the bus.
Daniel: Excuse me. Why are we going this way? This is not the route on the map. I am already late. I have a museum reservation at four.
Don Beto: Yes, sir. There is a {{b2}} procession on Avenida Universidad. The family is walking the coffin from the church to the cemetery. The whole street is closed for about twenty minutes.
Daniel: A funeral closes a public bus route?
Don Beto: Yes. It is normal here. The route is reopened as soon as the procession has passed. I will get you to Coyoacán about {{b3}} minutes late.
Daniel: I understand families want to mourn, but it is a working day. I have appointments. Is there really no way to make the family walk on a smaller street?
Don Beto: Sir, I will say this gently because you do not know our city yet. The family is not walking on Avenida Universidad to inconvenience you. They are walking on Avenida Universidad because the man who died lived on it for sixty years. He sold {{b4}} at the corner I just turned away from. Many of the people walking behind the coffin ate his tortas every day of their working lives. The street is theirs, in a sense your map does not show.
Daniel: But I am also using the street. I paid for the bus.
Don Beto: Yes. You paid forty pesos for a bus ride, and you have one. The {{b5}} is part of the bus ride today. It is not extra. The fare includes weather, traffic, and, sometimes, a funeral. None of us know in the morning which it will be.
Daniel: You sound as if you are defending it.
Don Beto: I am explaining it. I have driven this bus for nineteen years. In nineteen years I have been stopped about thirty times for funerals. I have been stopped about four thousand times for {{b6}}, two thousand times for political demonstrations, six hundred times for parties — quinceañeras, weddings — and twice for the President. The funerals are not the largest interruption. They are only the one that surprises tourists.
Daniel: I have not been rude. I just want to know why I am late.
Don Beto: Of course. You are late because, in this city, the {{b7}} are still allowed to use the road. Most cities have decided that the dead must use a different road — a side street, a hearse, a quiet hour. We have not decided that yet. Some people think we should. Others think we have not decided that because we do not want to. I am one of the others.
Daniel: And when you yourself die, will your family walk you down Avenida Universidad?
Don Beto: If they want to, and if the route is closed for them, yes. I expect a passenger that day will be annoyed because he is late to a museum. I hope my {{b8}} answers him the way I have answered you. Patiently, but without apologising.
Daniel: I am sorry I was sharp.
Don Beto: You were not sharp. You were on time. Time is what your country gave you. The funeral is what mine has given me. Both are {{b9}}, in a way. We are both lucky.
Narrator: Daniel arrived at the museum {{b10}} minutes late. He told the story that evening, at a small bar, to a Mexican friend. The friend asked him what he had said back to the driver. Daniel said: 'I thanked him.' The friend nodded. 'Then you got off the bus,' she said, 'in the city you came to {{b11}}.'

blanks.explanation: The city you came to see.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Don Beto says: 'Time is what your country gave you. The funeral is what mine has given me. Both are gifts.' What has your country given you that another country might find inconvenient? What has another country given that you would not want to give up if you lived there?
